questions.question: What is the only fundamental difference between an X-ray photon and a gamma-ray photon of the same energy?
options: The gamma ray travels faster
Their origin - X-rays come from electron interactions outside the nucleus, gamma rays from decay of the nucleus
The gamma ray has a shorter wavelength
The X-ray cannot expose film
questions.explanation: Photons of equal energy behave identically regardless of how they were produced; the X-ray/gamma distinction is purely one of origin.

questions.question: An operator raises the kVp while holding mA constant. Which set of effects follows?
options: Intensity rises; spectrum shape and maximum energy unchanged
Maximum photon energy rises, total intensity rises, the beam penetrates more, and subject contrast drops
Maximum photon energy rises but intensity falls
Only the exposure time changes
questions.explanation: kVp changes quality and quantity together: the spectrum's ceiling and effective energy shift upward, output climbs faster than the voltage, and the harder beam flattens contrast while widening latitude.

questions.question: A radiographer using Ir-192 needs a more penetrating beam for a thick section. What is the correct option?
options: Turn up the source activity control
Use a longer exposure time
Change to a higher-energy isotope such as Co-60, since gamma energies are fixed by the isotope
Crank the source further out of the projector
questions.explanation: A gamma source emits a line spectrum set by its nuclear structure - no control changes it. More activity or time changes only quantity, so a harder beam means a different isotope or an X-ray machine.

questions.question: On a processed radiograph, the area beneath an internal shrinkage void appears darker than the surrounding metal because:
options: The void contained moisture that developed the film
Less material lay in the beam path, so more radiation reached and blackened the film there
The void scattered radiation back toward the source
Voids emit their own characteristic radiation
questions.explanation: Radiography is differential absorption: removing absorber from the path increases the transmitted intensity, and more exposure produces more film density.

questions.question: A 0.12 in. focal spot radiographs a part with object-to-film distance 1.5 in. and source-to-object distance 18 in. The geometric unsharpness is about:
questions.explanation: Ug = F·t/d = 0.12 × 1.5 ÷ 18 = 0.010 in. - comfortably inside a typical 0.020 in. code cap for thin sections.

questions.question: A 0.75 in. feature sits 30 in. from the source with the film at 36 in. Its image on the film measures about:
questions.explanation: Image = object × SFD/SOD = 0.75 × 36 ÷ 30 = 0.90 in. The diverging beam always enlarges the shadow.

questions.question: Which combination produces the sharpest radiographic image?
options: Large source, short source-to-object distance, film spaced off the part
Small source, long source-to-object distance, film in contact with the part
Small source, short source-to-object distance, film spaced off the part
Large source, long source-to-object distance, film in contact with the part
questions.explanation: Ug = F·t/d shrinks with a smaller source size F, a longer source-to-object distance d, and a shorter object-to-film distance t - the three geometric levers of sharpness.

questions.question: During a tube-shift depth check with the same source shift, flaw A's image moves 0.10 in. and flaw B's image moves 0.40 in. What does this tell you?
options: Flaw B lies higher above the film, nearer the source side, than flaw A
Flaw B lies closer to the film than flaw A
Flaw B is four times larger than flaw A
The two exposures were made at different kVp settings
questions.explanation: Parallax shift grows with height above the film: features near the film barely move, features near the source side move most - so the larger shift marks the shallower (source-side) flaw.

questions.question: Which statement about lead intensifying screens is correct?
options: They intensify at all energies, including low-kV work
They emit light that exposes the film
Above roughly 125 kV they give a net intensification of about 2-4× while also filtering low-energy scatter
They are used only to protect the film from pressure marks
questions.explanation: Lead foil intensifies by photoelectron emission - a net gain only above about 125 kV - and simultaneously improves image quality by preferentially absorbing oblique, lower-energy scattered radiation.

questions.question: A radiograph shows a small region that is noticeably fuzzier than the rest of the image, with no part geometry to explain it. The most likely cause is:
options: The kVp was set too high
Poor screen-to-film contact in that region - a gap let electrons spread before reaching the film
The source decayed during the exposure
Excessive developer temperature
questions.explanation: Electrons and light diffuse sideways across any screen-to-film gap, so a local loss of contact prints as localized unsharpness - the reason cassettes use pressure pads and vacuum designs, and why wavy screens are retired.
